coredata.dc:title: Green carbon dots derived from Atractylodes macrocephala: A potential nanodrug for treating alcoholic gastric ulcer
coredata.dc:creator: Zhai, Changming
Lu, Fang
Du, Xin
Zhang, Mengling
Zhang, Yue
Ma, Yurong
Zhao, Yan
Huang, Hui
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Alcoholic gastric ulcer is a common acute gastric injury disease. The drugs currently used in clinical practice not only cannot fundamentally treat gastric injury, but also have serious side effects. There is an urgent demand for the discovery of a mild drug to treat alcoholic gastric ulcers. Herein, the green carbon dots derived from charred Atractylodes macrocephala (CAM-CDs) were acquired and have been proven to be safe and effective in alleviating alcoholic gastric ulcers at an inhibition rate up to 60%. CAM-CDs can markedly attenuate the gastric mucosa damage such as mucosal defect, bleeding and inflammatory cell infiltration by histopathological examination. Serum and tissue inflammatory cytokine measurements, as well as immunohistochemistry results, indicate that its mechanism of gastric mucosal protection may involve the reduction of IL-1β and TNF-α by regulating inflammatory signaling pathway of the NF-κB/NLRP3 axis, as well as elevation of IL-10 levels. CAM-CDs also can reduce oxidative stress markers (MDA), increase PGE2 and mucin secretion (MUC5AC), and it simultaneously exerts slight inhibition of hydrogen potassium ATPase and pepsin activity to protect gastric mucosa, as well as increases the microbial diversity and regulates species composition of gut microbiota in rats with gastric ulcer. Our work provides a new perspective on utilizing carbon-based nanomaterials in the development of new mild drugs.

Black arrow heads indicate gastric mucosal epithelial damage, red arrow heads indicate inflammatory cell infiltration (m: mucosa and sm: submucosa).

Effects of CAM-CDs on inflammatory cytokines (A) Interleukin-6 (IL-6); (B) Interleukin-10 (IL-10) level; (C) interleukin-1β (IL-1β); (D) Tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α). Ran, ranitidine control at 40 mg/kg; OME, omeprazole control at 20 mg/kg; CAM-CDs treated groups: H, high dose of 26.88 mg/kg; M, medium dose of 6.72 mg/kg; L, low dose of 2.24 mg/kg. (mean ± SD, n = 6). Statistical significance: * **P < 0.001, * *P < 0.01, *P < 0.05 vs model group. ###P < 0.001, #P < 0.05 vs normal group.

CAM-CDs treated groups: H, high dose of 26.88 mg/kg; M, medium dose of 6.72 mg/kg. Values are expressed as Median (quartile). Statistical significance: ***P < 0.001；**P < 0.01 vs. model group. ##P < 0.01 vs. normal group.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alcoholic gastric ulcer is a common acute gastric injury disease. The drugs currently used in clinical practice not only cannot fundamentally treat gastric injury, but also have serious side effects. There is an urgent demand for the discovery of a mild drug to treat alcoholic gastric ulcers. Herein, the green carbon dots derived from charred Atractylodes macrocephala (CAM-CDs) were acquired and have been proven to be safe and effective in alleviating alcoholic gastric ulcers at an inhibition rate up to 60%. CAM-CDs can markedly attenuate the gastric mucosa damage such as mucosal defect, bleeding and inflammatory cell infiltration by histopathological examination. Serum and tissue inflammatory cytokine measurements, as well as immunohistochemistry results, indicate that its mechanism of gastric mucosal protection may involve the reduction of IL-1β and TNF-α by regulating inflammatory signaling pathway of the NF-κB/NLRP3 axis, as well as elevation of IL-10 levels. CAM-CDs also can reduce oxidative stress markers (MDA), increase PGE2 and mucin secretion (MUC5AC), and it simultaneously exerts slight inhibition of hydrogen potassium ATPase and pepsin activity to protect gastric mucosa, as well as increases the microbial diversity and regulates species composition of gut microbiota in rats with gastric ulcer. Our work provides a new perspective on utilizing carbon-based nanomaterials in the development of new mild drugs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As life pressures mount and the pace of life accelerates, the annual incidence of alcohol-related diseases, particularly gastric ulcers, is on the rise [1,2]. Oral consumption of excessive alcohol directly damages gastric mucosal epithelial cells, impairs microcirculation, triggers oxidative stress reactions, and induces an elevation in inflammatory cytokines, ultimately resulting in gastric ulcers [3]. Under current clinical drug treatment, gastric ulcers still exhibit high recurrence rates and often require prolonged and costly maintenance therapy. Improper use of medications has resulted in unexpected side effects, among which the disruption of gut microbiota homeostasis has been particularly emphasized nowadays [4–6]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore new and gentle drugs for the treatment of this disease.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a rising star in the carbon-based nanomaterial family [7,8], have been widely used in nanobiotechnology and biomedical fields due to its excellent properties, such as water dispersibility, excellent photoluminescence, biocompatibility, good chemical stability and low toxicity [7,9,10]. Our team found that a variety of CDs from herbs had such characteristics as hemostasis, anti-inflammatory, sedative, hypoglycemic, antioxidant and other biological effects, proving a tremendous potential in the field of medical research [11–14]. However, researches on the intrinsic self-bioactivities and potential pharmacological effects of CDs are limited and still in the early stages.

Through over 2000 years of clinical practice, Chinese medical experts have discovered that charcoal processing can enhance or alter the efficacy of numerous herbal medicines [15,16], which is represented by Atractylodis Macrocephalae Koidz. (AM). Compared with AM, charred AM obtained by the heating and carbonization process is more conducive to hemostasis, gastric mucosal protection and ulcer healing, [17,18] supported by consecutive clinical evidence and modern medical researches [19]. However, its underlying material basis is still unrevealed. Our preliminary research found that semi-carbonized nanodots from charred Atractylodes macrocephala have a protective effect against stress-induced gastric mucosal lesions through modulating neurotransmitters release and organism metabolism [18]. Based on this, we improved the preparation method of CDs in this study and subsequently investigated and evaluated its effect on alcohol-induced gastric ulcers, which is the most commonly used gastric ulcer models worldwide, mainly focusing on its effects on inflammatory pathways and other related mechanisms, as well as their role in modulating the gut microbiota.

In our experiment, the CAM-CDs effectively reduced the alcoholic gastric ulcer area proportion and ulcer index, while increasing the ulcer inhibition rate by up to 60% compared with positive drugs of omeprazole (43.11%) and ranitidine (36.19%). CAM-CDs mainly protected the integrity of gastric mucosa through reducing gastric tissue inflammation, alleviating oxidative stress, increasing PGE2 and mucoprotein secretion, and also slightly inhibiting the activity of hydrogen potassium ATPase and pepsin, as well as maintaining intestinal microflora homeostasis. These findings provide more comprehensive evidence for its potential as an anti-ulcer nanodrug.

Charred Atractylodis Macrocephalae Koidz. (CAM) was acquired from Beijing Qiancao Herbal Pieces Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 1000 Da MW cut-off dialysis membranes were procured from Beijing Ruida Henghui Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Microporous membrane (0.22 µm) was offered by Tianjin Jinteng Experimental Equipment Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The analytical-grade chemical reagents were sourced from Beijing Chemical Industry Group Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All experiments were conducted with deionized water.

The experiments on animals were approved by the Animal Experiment Ethics Committee of Beijing University of Traditional Chinese Medicine (IRB: BUCM-4–2022112703–4078). In our work, the SD male rats of SPF grade (provided by Beijing SiPeiFu Biotechnology Co., Ltd.), weighing 200 ± 10.0 g were utilized. The animals were maintained under the temperature of 24.0 ± 1.0 ℃; relative humidity, 55–65% in a 12 h light/dark cycle. Rats were provided with sterilized water and normal diet at ad libitum access.

Initially, a quantity of 500 g of Atractylodes macrocephala (AM) was introduced into crucibles and subjected to calcination within a muffle furnace at 70 °C for 30 min. After that, the precursors were heated up to 350 °C and maintained for 70 min to obtain CAM, with a natural cooling process. CAM was subsequently pulverized into powder, boiled for 1 h twice using distilled water. The obtained yellowish brown solution was then subjected to a filtration step utilizing a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membrane, concentrated under reduced pressure and purified by a 1000 Da dialysis membrane to acquire CAM-CDs.

The CCK-8 assay was employed to evaluate cellular cytotoxicity. HL-7702 cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 (90%) with FBS (10%) medium in a 96-well plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells per 100 μL/well at 37 ℃ in 5% CO2. After 24 h incubation, the cells were treated with different concentrations of 100 μL CAM-CDs solution and re-incubated for 12 h or 24 h. The 96-well plate was then washed by PBS and CCK-8 solution was added (100 μL per well). After incubated for an additional 4 h, 96-well plate was placed in a microplate reader to measure optical density at 450 nm in triplicate, independently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alcoholic gastric ulcer model and therapeutic intervention
ce:section.ce:para: The gastric ulcer model induced by alcohol was established using the conventional method with certain modifications [20,21]. The administration concentration of the positive drug was chosen according to the references [22–24], while the administration concentrations of CAM-CDs were determined based on previous reports on carbon dots derived from herbs [25–27] and our preliminary experiments to select safe and effective dosages. Rats were randomly assigned into 7 groups including normal, model, and drug treated groups of positive control of omeprazole (OME, 20 mg/kg)[23], positive control of ranitidine (Ran, 40 mg/kg)[22,24] and CAM-CDs treated groups (26.88 mg/kg for high dose (H) [25], 6.72 mg/kg for medium dose (M)[26], 2.24 mg/kg for Low dose (L)[27], respectively). The normal and model group were given deionized water, and the control groups and each drug group were given the corresponding liquid by gavage every day. On the fifth day, 2 h after intragastric administration, all groups were given 95% ethanol (1 mL/100 g) by gavage except normal group. After 1 h, the feces of rats were collected and put into a sterile cryopreservation tube for cryopreservation in liquid nitrogen. After pentobarbital sodium anesthesia, blood of rats was taken from celiac aorta and rats were sacrificed. The stomachs of rats were cut out and open along the greater curvature to be inspected and collected. Images of the injured surface of gastric mucosa were captured by digital camera and stereomicroscope. Then, half of the gastric tissue was cut off and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde fixative, and the other half of the gastric tissue was frozen in a cryopreservation tube.
The visible lesion of gastric mucosa was evaluated by two reported methods with some modifications [28,29]:

We utilized ImageJ software (64-bit Java 1.8.0) to measure the gastric ulcer area on digital camera images. The ulcer area proportion (%) = ulcer area/ total stomach area× 100. Ulcer inhibition rate (%) = (mean of ulcer area proportion model group − ulcer area proportion treated group )/ mean of ulcer area proportion model group × 100.

Manual scoring method was also used to calculate the ulcer grade from images recorded under the electronic stereomicroscope. The methods was as follows: the complete gastric mucosa was 0 point; 0.5 point for point-like damage; If the damage was strip like and the length was > 1 mm, 1 point would be given for every 1 mm; If the damage was massive and width > 1 mm, the measured area was scored. Ulcer lesion index of each group was obtained by the cumulation of all the points. The anti-ulcer indexes of drugs were calculated by formula: anti-ulcer index = (mean of ulcer lesion index model group - ulcer lesion index treated group )/ mean of ulcer lesion index model group .
The paraffin sections of longitudinal gastric tissues were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and observed under light microscope.

Immunohistochemical methods were employed using NF-κB P65(Abcam, UK), caspase-1 (Abcam, UK) and NLRP3 (Abcam, UK) antibodies to investigate the inflammatory features of each group. Immunohistochemical staining procedures for gastric tissue were performed according to the protocols reported [30].

The gastric tissues were homogenized with a ninefold volume of normal saline water and subsequently centrifugate. The supernatant was diluted with PBS buffer as required. The levels of Malondialdehyde (MDA), Hydrogen potassium ATPase (H+, K+- ATPase) and Pepsin in rat stomach tissue were detected according to the test instruction of each kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, Nanjing, China).
ELISA assay was conducted to measure IL-6, IL-10 levels in serum, and TNF-α, IL-1β, MUC5AC and PGE2 levels in gastric tissue following the certain instructions of ELISA kit (Abcam, Cambridge, UK).

Firstly, the microbial community DNA in rat feces samples were extracted. Then the variable v4 region of 16 S rDNA was selected, and the reaction parameters were set to enter the PCR thermal cycle for amplification. The amplified product was purified, and a library was established, which was sequenced by Hiseq 2500 (BGI, Shenzhen, China).

Statistical analysis was conducted using the software SPSS (version 20.0). The data were presented as the mean ± standard deviation. Differences between the results were assessed using one-way ANOVA. Statistical significance was considered when the P value was less than 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and cytotoxicity test of CAM-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A displays the transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CAM-CDs. As shown, the obtained CAM-CDs are well dispersed with the size ranged from 1.5 to 4 nm and the crystal lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, correlating with the < 100 > lattice plane of graphitic carbon [31]. The UV-Visible spectrum is absorption and PL spectra of CAM-CDs are presented in Fig. 1B. In the UV-Visible spectrum of CAM-CDs (black line), the peak located around at 268 nm is attributed to π − π * transition [7]. Moreover, the CAM-CDs solution exhibits an optimum emission peak at 456 nm when excited at 357 nm. The XPS spectra of CAM-CDs are shown in Fig. 1C and D. The full-scan spectrum reveals that CAM-CDs are composed of C and O elements with contents of 73.93% and 26.07%, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s shows three prominent peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.2 eV, corresponding to C–C/C–H, C–O, and CO, respectively, indicating that CAM-CDs are rich in surface active functional groups, including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [32].

Subsequently, its cytotoxicity was assessed, and the selection of the concentration range was based on our previous experiments and relevant references [11,33]. As shown in Fig. 1E, CAM-CDs promoted cell proliferation after 12 h administration at concentrations ranging from 7.8125 µg/mL to 31.25 µg/mL, and exerted no significant influence on cell viability ranging from 31.25 µg/mL to 2000 µg/mL. As can be seen from Fig. 1F, after 24 h administration of CAM-CDs at concentration ranged from 7.8125 µg/mL to 500 µg/mL, no significant decrease in cell viability was observed. While cell viability was about 75–80% when the concentration of CAM-CDs increased from 1000 to 2000 µg/mL. To sum up, CAM-CDs exhibited no toxicity to HL-7702 cells and possessed outstanding biocompatibility below the concentration of 500 µg/mL. The results indicated that its safe dosage was not lower than that of other reported green CDs, and it even exhibited stronger safety properties [34,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alleviating effect of CAM-CDs on gastric mucosa injury
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of CAM-CDs administration on alcoholic gastric injury was studied in following experiment. Gastric mucosal images were taken by camera and stereomicroscope. First-line drugs in clinical treatment for stress gastric ulcer (omeprazole and ranitidine) were used as the positive control in this experiment. The gastric damage area proportion in photographs ( Fig. 2A) was measured by Image J software automatically (Fig. 2C) while the ulcer index of images captured by stereomicroscope (Fig. 2B) was acquired by manual counting (Fig. 2D and Table S1). Compared with the intact gastric mucosa in normal rats, alcohol gavage led to obvious damage of gastric mucosa in model group including severe mucosal edema, spasm and extremely bleeding, with higher ulcer area proportion and index (P < 0.001) (Fig. 2C and D).

Compared with model group, both omeprazole group (OME) and ranitidine group (RAN) show lower gastric mucosal ulcer area proportion and lesion index (P < 0.001), especially in ranitidine group with distinct ulcer inhibition rate of 43.11% and anti-ulcer index of 0.23. The gastric ulcer in all the CAM-CDs administration groups (H, M and L: high, medium and low doses of CAM-NCs) were alleviated compared with model group (P < 0.001). Among them, the ulcer inhibition rate (60.02%) and anti-ulcer index (0.39) of M group were the highest, which indicated the best efficacy of CAM-CDs at medium dose and its excellent anti-ulcer effect was even stronger than positive drugs of omeprazole and ranitidine (P < 0.05). In M group, there was almost no massive bleeding and ulcer sites were only scattered. Both software and manual scoring results for quantification of gastric mucosal damage were consistent with each other. The experimental results indicate a great anti-gastric ulcer effect of CAM-CDs which is consistent with the ancient records and usages of Chinese herbal medicine [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CAM-CDs on pathological characteristics of gastric tissue in alcohol-induced ulcer rats
ce:section.ce:para: The histopathological photomicrographs of the stomach in rats are shown in Fig. 3. As shown, the gastric mucosa of the normal group (Fig. 3A) is smooth, with clear boundary of each layer of tissue and well-arranged epithelial cells without mucosal defect. In the model group (Fig. 3B), the gastric mucosa was seriously damaged, with obvious loose mucosa layers, exfoliated and necrotized surface epithelial cells, infiltrated inflammatory cells and submucosal edema. The blood vessels of gastric tissues dilated and congested, and blood cells spilled to all layers of gastric mucosa. In contrast to the model group, all the drug treated groups (Fig. 3C-G) possess improved gastric mucosa. In ranitidine (Ran, Fig. 3C) and omeprazole (OME, Fig. 3D) treated groups, the gastric mucosal epithelial cells were relatively orderly arranged and the defect tearing and shedding were reduced. With treatment of CAM-CDs (Fig. 3E-F), the bleeding condition and inflammatory cell infiltration of stomach in rats were mitigated. It can be seen from the experimental results that the medium and high dose groups of CAM-CDs have strong protective effect on gastric mucosa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CAM-CDs on inflammatory cytokines and regulation of NF-κB / NLRP3 axis
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of CAM-CDs on the oxidative stress markers and inflammatory factors of rats, including IL-6, IL-10 in serum and MDA, TNF-α, and IL-1β in tissues which will change in occurrence of gastric ulcer were further evaluated.

Excessive consumption of alcohol can activate the autoimmune system, which will release a multitude of inflammatory cytokines, including IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and so on, inducing gastric mucosal injury [3,37,38]. On the contrary, IL-10 plays an important role in downregulating the inflammatory cascade and preventing diseases [37]. As shown in Fig. 4, IL-6 level in the serum of model group (105.4 ± 13.04 pg/mL), as well as IL-1β (36.53 ± 4.49 pg/mL) and TNF-α (43.3 ± 5.01 pg/mL) levels in the stomach tissue of model group are all significantly elevated (P < 0.001) in contrast with those in normal group (IL-6: 24.04 ± 9.562 pg/mL; IL-1β: 20.23 ± 6.75 pg/mL; TNF-α: 26.58 ± 9.17 pg/mL). In comparison to the model group, the positive control of ranitidine could significantly decrease IL-6 (44.91 ± 11.40 pg/mL, P < 0.001) and IL-1β level of rats. As for TNF-α level, ranitidine only downregulated it in a tendency without statistical differences with model group. The other positive control of omeprazole could significantly decrease IL-6 (57.53 ± 9.643 pg/mL). In contrast to the model group, CAM-CDs administration produced a significant decrease of IL-6 level (group of L: 46.97 ± 16.41 pg/mL, P < 0.001; M: 41.62 ± 3.841 pg/mL, P < 0.001; H:42.99 ± 5.749 pg/mL, P < 0.001), IL-1β (group of L:15.32 ± 7.08 pg/mL, P < 0.001; M: 22.46 ± 11.70 pg/mL, P > 0.05; H: 14.45 ± 4.15 pg/mL, P < 0.001) and TNF-α (group of L: 31.43 ± 5.79 pg/mL, P < 0.05; M: 33.48 ± 1.69 pg/mL, P > 0.05; H: 31.41 ± 7.98 pg/mL, P < 0.05). There was no statistically significant difference in IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α among groups of OME, RAN and CAM-CDs at different dosages. When comparing IL-10 level in stomach tissue, which was decreased significantly by ethanol intake in model group (23.44 ± 1.032 pg/mL, P < 0.001) compared with normal group (97.74 ± 9.12 pg/mL), all drugs of positive control including omeprazole group (106.6 ± 57.41 pg/mL, P < 0.05), ranitidine group (100.5 ± 43.00 pg/mL, P < 0.01) and high-dose CAM-CDs group (142.1 ± 31.15 pg/mL, P < 0.001) manifested to upregulate IL-10 level over that of the model group. Among all these drugs, including OME, RAN and CAM-CDs, CAM-CDs at high concentration show the best behavior in elevating the defense factor of gastric ulcer inflammation of IL-10, with significant differences with RAN, L and M group (P < 0.05).

To further investigate the inhibitory mechanisms of CAM-CDs on ulcer-related inflammatory responses, we employed immunohistochemical methods to assess alterations in inflammatory mediators along the NF-κB/NLRP3 pathway.

The inflammasome, which is a component of innate immunity and the inflammatory response, plays a critical role in the pathogenesis and progression of gastric impairment caused by ethanol. It has been acknowledged that suppressing the activity of nucleotide binding domain-like receptor family pyrin domain-containing 3 (NLRP3) is an effective mechanism for countering ethanol-induced gastric mucosa impairment [39,40]. The activation of inflammatory NLRP3 involves initiation through pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) such as TLR4 and RAGE, or any factor capable of triggering nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-κB) activation [39]. This initiation leads to an upregulation of NLRP3, pro-IL-1β, and pro-TNF-α expression, ultimately resulting in pathological damage [40]. As shown in Fig. 5, the gastric level of NF-κB p65 exhibited a substantial increase in the model group in comparison to the normal group (P < 0.001). Conversely, the ranitidine, medium-dose, and high-dose CAM-CDs groups demonstrated a noteworthy reduction (P < 0.05) in NF-κB p65 levels when compared to the model group. The omeprazole and low-dose CAM-CDs groups displayed an insignificant alteration (P > 0.05) in NF-κB p65 levels relative to the model group. Ethanol exhibited a significant elevation in caspase-1 levels compared to the normal group (P < 0.001), while omeprazole, low-dose, and medium-dose CAM-CDs interventions all demonstrated a remarkable reduction in caspase-1 expression (P < 0.05). In the model group, NLRP3 expression exhibited a significant increase compared to the normal group (P < 0.001). Following drug intervention, NLRP3 levels consistently decreased in each treatment group, except for the low-dose CAM-CDs group, and a clear dose-response relationship was observed (P < 0.05). These findings suggest that the inhibition of the NF-κB / NLRP3 axis, to some extent, contributes to the ameliorative effects of CAM-CDs on ethanol-induced gastric ulcers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-effects of CAM-CDs for alcohol-induced gastric ulcer
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, we quantified the enzymatic activity of H+ -K+ -ATPase, serving as a representative indicator of gastric acid secretion, and evaluated the levels of mucosal protective factors, such as PGE2 and MUC5AC.

The oxidative stress marker of MDA in gastric tissue of rats was measured. As illustrated in Fig. 6A, the MDA content in the tissue of rats of model group (4.00 ± 0.48 nmol/mg prot) was significantly increased (P < 0.001) compared to the normal group (1.68 ± 0.50 nmol/mg prot). When compared with the model group, MDA content in tissue of the rats in OME group (2.68 ± 0.47 nmol/mg prot), Ran group (2.06 ± 0.45 nmol/mg prot), high-dose of CAM-CDs group (2.39 ± 0.37 nmol/mg prot) and medium dose of CAM-CDs group (2.11 ± 0.39 nmol/mg prot) were all significantly decreased (P < 0.001). There is no significant difference among positive drug groups and CAM-CDs groups at high and medium doses on MDA levels, while the MDA level in RAN was significantly lower than that in L.

As the key enzyme participating in gastric acid secretion, H+-K+-ATPase was also assayed in this study (Fig. 6B). By contrast with the normal rats (2.63 ± 0.40 U/mg prot), the activity of H+-K+-ATPase in gastric tissue of model rats (3.18 ± 0.60 U/mg prot) slightly increased without significant difference. In contrast to the model rats, the activity of H+-K+-ATPase in medium dose of CAM-CDs group (2.24 ± 0.28 U/mg prot) was decreased (P < 0.05). No significant differences were observed among the other groups.

Pepsin is an aggressive factor of gastric mucosa. Intragastric infusion with alcohol in model rats induced a significant decrease of pepsin (9.08 ± 2.17 U/mg prot, P < 0.05, Fig. 6C), by contrast with the normal group (6.29 ± 1.02 U/mg prot). The pepsin activity could be significantly reduced (P < 0.01) by pretreatment with ranitidine (5.84 ± 0.97 U/mg prot) and medium dose of CAM-CDs (5.47 ± 0.90 U/mg prot). No significant differences were observed among positive drugs and CAM-CDs groups. The above results of our experiments show that ranitidine is more effective than omeprazole in treatment of our gastric ulcer model (Fig. 2, Fig. 4A, B, Fig. 6A-C), so the latter experiment mainly used ranitidine as the positive control drug.

Besides the aggressive factors against gastric mucosa, the defense factors in gastric ulcer were further measured. PGE2 plays a key role in maintaining the integrity of mucous membrane cells by improving blood flow and enhancing the resistance of gastric mucosa to damage [38]. As shown in Fig. 6D, the content of PGE2 in gastric tissue of model rats (38.45 ± 9.78 pg/mL) was significantly decreased (P < 0.001), by comparing with normal group (99.03 ± 24.98 pg/mL). In comparison to the model group, the PGE2 content in gastric tissue in Ran group (89.07 ± 36.11 pg/mL, P < 0.01), low dose (76.10 ± 12.33 pg/mL, P < 0.05) and high dose of CAM-CDs group (79.61 ± 10.82 pg/mL, P < 0.05) were significantly higher than that in model group. No significant differences was observed among positive drug and CAM-CDs groups at different concentrations.

When gastrointestinal diseases occur, MUC5AC is the most sensitive and significant marker secreted from mucus layer as a vital defense factor of gastric mucosa [41]. Fig. 6E shows that the MUC5AC level of gastric tissue in the model group (0.19 ± 0.20 ng/mL) was significantly lower than that in normal group (0.98 ± 0.19 ng/mL, P < 0.001). Compared with model group, MUC5AC level in medium dose of CAM-CDs group (0.88 ± 0.24 ng/mL) was significantly increased (P < 0.001), while the level in ranitidine group (0.46 ± 0.29 ng/mL) only showed a trend of increasing without any statistical difference. The MUC5AC level in group of CAM-CDs at medium dose was significantly higher than that in RAN, L and H groups. These results indicate that CAM-CDs could significantly enhance self-defense ability of gastric mucosa by increasing the PGE2 and MUC5AC factors, and CAM-CDs at medium dose was even more effective than positive drug.

In conclusion, CAM-CDs could protect gastric mucosa on the one hand by reducing the attack factors including inflammation, oxidative stress and slightly inhibiting the activities of H+-K+-ATPase and pepsin; on the other hand, by increasing the defense factors of PGE2 and mucin secretion when alcoholic gastric ulcer occur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CAM-CDs on intestinal microflora
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, overuse of anti-ulcer drugs such as proton pump inhibitors (PPI) has been reported to destroy the homeostasis of intestinal flora of patients [5]. Thus, to evaluate the role of anti-ulcer drugs in the development of microbiota is necessary. 16 S rDNA genome sequencing for intestinal flora was carried out in this study.

Venn diagram ( Fig. 7A) shows the overlap of 693 OTUs coexisted in all 4 groups. Model group only shares 88 OTUs with normal group; in comparison, CAM-CDs groups share more OTUs with normal group (up to 113 OTUs in medium dose of CAM-CDs group; and 109 in high dose of CAM-CDs group, respectively).

Partial least squares discrimination analysis (PLS-DA) is used to determine whether grouped samples have differences. As shown in the Fig. 7B, the model group and the normal group are mainly separated in the positive and negative directions of the vertical axis. Groups of CAM-CDs (M and H) are in the same direction as the normal group on the vertical axis, and the high dose of CAM-CDs group overlaps with the normal group to a greater extent, indicating the smallest difference between the high dose of CAM-CDs group and the normal group.

Species abundance at genus level is shown in Fig. 7C and Table S2. By comparing the model group with the normal group, it can be found that alcohol gavage significantly increased the relative abundance of 5 genera of intestinal microflora (Helicobacter, Akkermansia, Prevotella, Bacteroides, Coprococcus) and decreased the relative abundance of 5 genera (Ruminococcus, Lactobacillus, Allobaculum, Sutterella, 02d06). However, administration of CAM-CDs at high and medium dose reversed most of the above changes (5 genera including Helicobacter, Akkermansia, Lactobacillus, Coprococcus, Sutterella), and administration of medium dose of CAM-CDs could also reverse other 4 genera changes including Allobaculum, Prevotella, Ruminococcus, 02d06. Species abundance at genus level in medium dose of CAM-CDs group is the most similar to that in the normal group. In addition, compared with normal group, the relative abundances of Roseburia, Oscillospira, and Blautia in the medium dose of CAM-CDs group were significantly increased, which may contribute to the production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and provide potential health benefits [42,43].

Species abundance heatmap at genus level is shown in Fig. 7D. The branch length between CAM-CDs administrated groups (M and H) and normal group were shorter than that between CAM-CDs administrated groups (M and H) and model group, indicating CAM-CDs can adjust the species composition and abundance of gut microbiota to be similar with that of normal group.

The diversity of microbial community composition in the samples could be measured by Alpha diversity analysis including indexes like Shannon, Simpson, OBS, Chao and ACE. In the analysis of Shannon, OBS, Chao and ACE indexes ( Fig. 8), the larger the four indexes are, the higher the alpha diversity of microflora will be. The results show that compared with the normal group, Shannon, OBS, Chao and ACE indexes of the model group have a downward trend. However, in comparison to that in the model group, CAM-CDs groups at high and medium dose could elevate these indexes. As for Simpson index, the smaller it is, the higher the diversity within the sample will be, which analysis results are shown in the Fig. 8B. Compared with the Simpson index of the normal group, the model group was significantly increased (P < 0.01). Simpson index of CAM-CDs groups at medium dose group and high dose were significantly decreased (P < 0.001). The results showed that alcohol could reduce the alpha diversity of intestinal microflora. However, CAM-CDs mitigated this negative impact of alcohol on the alpha diversity of gut microbiota. High diversity is generally considered as an indicator of healthy microbiota, and decreased diversity is often associated with poor health related to several diseases [44,45]. These results suggested CAM-CDs could increase the microbial diversity and regulate species composition of gut microbiota in rats with gastric ulcer, thereby enhancing the host's resistance to adverse interference by external factors on microbial homeostasis or gastrointestinal infection. The positive modulation of CAM-CDs on gut microbiota stability endows it with immense potential and significant advantages as a new nanodrug for gastric ulcers, for the disruption of gut microbiota homeostasis caused by current frontline therapies for gastric ulcers remains a challenging side effect and drawback [6,46]. In this aspect, our nanodrug of CAM-CDs holds promise in surpassing numerous commonly used medications, warranting further investigation and development.

In our study, CAM-CDs, the green carbon dots derived from the medicinal herb can effectively mitigate alcoholic gastric injuries with an ulcer inhibition rate up to 60%. Its mechanism to protect gastric mucosa may include reducing the inflammatory reactions by inhibiting the NF-κB/NLRP3 signaling pathway, alleviating oxidative stress injury in gastric tissue, and decreasing gastric enzyme activity, thereby reducing the invasion of attack factors on the gastric mucosa. On the other hand, CAM-CDs can increase the levels of gastric mucosal defense factors of IL-10, PGE2, and MUC5AC. In addition, through the preliminary sequencing of intestinal flora, we found that CAM-CDs could regulate the alpha diversity as well as species composition and abundance of intestinal flora. Our work provides a new perspective on utilizing carbon-based nanomaterials in the development of new mild drugs.
